figures.caption: Figure 7: M.5: A gated memory branch reads from P and contributes through a content-dependent gate. At initialisation bg < 0 keeps the gate nearly closed, preserving the frozen self-attention path.

Figure 10: Absolute forgetting curves on LoCoMo for the GPT-2 (124M) backbone at 1× capacity. Three methods (M.2, M.4, M.6) produce substantial retained-memory scores; three (M.1, M.3, M.5) are indistinguishable from the baseline. Higher and flatter curves indicate stronger memory.

Figure 9: Two learning phases. Type 1 trains the memory adapter parameters offline by backpropagation through the frozen decoder. Type 2 updates the persistent memory online at each conversation turn with all model weights frozen.

Figure 2: Three decoder-only injection strategies. (a) KV prefix prepends memory-derived keys and values to the self-attention cache. (b) Parallel cross-attention inserts a new attention pathway alongside the existing self-attention. (c) Gated branch computes a memory readout and adds it through a learned content-dependent gate.

Figure 12: Cumulative knowledge score Ks over 30 sessions at 1× capacity. Successful methods (M.2, M.4, M.6) accumulate knowledge substantially above the baseline; failing methods (M.1, M.3, M.5, not shown for clarity) produce ∆K < 0.2.

Figure 11: Absolute forgetting curves at 10× capacity. All six methods now produce substantial retained-memory scores. Methods that failed at 1× (M.1, M.3, M.5) converge to comparable performance with the strong-bias methods, demonstrating that the 1× gap is architectural, not fundamental.
